mnemonics.title: Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat
mnemonics.content: GBN: Go Back and Nuke (retransmit all from error). SR: Select and Repeat (only the error).

mnemonics.title: Stop-and-Wait Window Size
mnemonics.content: Wait for ACK before sending the next packet - Window = 1.

mnemonics.title: Sequence Number Bits
mnemonics.content: GBN: 2^k - 1; SR: 2^(k-1) - remember for Selective, think 'k-1 bits for half the sequence space'.

acronyms.usage: Protocol that automatically retransmits corrupted or lost frames
Control frame sent by receiver to confirm successful frame receipt
Control frame indicating frame error or loss (optional in some protocols)
Used in TCP to acknowledge non-contiguous data blocks
A bit-oriented Data Link Layer protocol supporting flow control

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a window frame sliding across a row of sequence numbers. As ACKs arrive, the left edge moves right, allowing new frames to enter from the right. The window 'slides' across the sequence number space.
Draw a timeline: Sender → Frame → [Propagation] → Receiver → [Processing] → ACK → [Propagation] → Sender. The sender is blocked during the entire round trip.
Show frames 0-5 sent, frame 2 corrupted. Draw arrows showing frames 2,3,4,5 all being retransmitted. Visual makes clear why it 'goes back'.

analogies.analogy: Like sending letters by mail and waiting for delivery confirmation before sending the next letter. Very reliable but slow.
Like a pizza delivery person who can deliver multiple pizzas in one trip around a neighborhood. Can carry several pizzas (window size) before needing to return to the pizzeria (get ACKs).
Like handing someone a package (data) while also giving them a note confirming receipt of their previous package (ACK) - combining two tasks in one trip.
